content.problemStatement:
The city of Siruseri is impeccably planned. The city is divided into a rectangular array of cells with MMM rows and NNN columns. Each cell has a metro station. There is one train running left to right and back along each row, and one running top to bottom and back along each column. Each trains starts at some time TTT and goes back and forth along its route (a row or a column) forever.

Ordinary trains take two units of time to go from one station to the next. There are some fast trains that take only one unit of time to go from one station to the next. Finally, there are some slow trains that take three units of time to go from one station the next. You may assume that the halting time at any station is negligible.

Here is a description of a metro system with 333 rows and 444 columns:

 

`         S(1)  F(2)  O(2)  F(4)
  F(3)    .     .     .     .
  S(2)    .     .     .     .
  O(2)    .     .     .     .
` 

The label at the beginning of each row/column indicates the type of train (F for fast, O for ordinary, S for slow) and its starting time. Thus, the train that travels along row 1 is a fast train and it starts at time 333. It starts at station (111, 111) and moves right, visiting the stations along this row at times 3,4,53, 4, 53,4,5 and 666 respectively. It then returns back visiting the stations from right to left at times 6,7,86, 7, 86,7,8 and 999. It again moves right now visiting the stations at times 9,10,119, 10, 119,10,11 and 121212, and so on. Similarly, the train along column 333 is an ordinary train starting at time 222. So, starting at the station (333,111), it visits the three stations on column 333 at times 2,42, 42,4 and 666, returns back to the top of the column visiting them at times 6,86,86,8 and 101010, and so on.

Given a starting station, the starting time and a destination station, your task is to determine the earliest time at which one can reach the destination using these trains.

For example suppose we start at station (222,333) at time 888 and our aim is to reach the station (111,111). We may take the slow train of the second row at time 888 and reach (222,444) at time 111111. It so happens that at time 111111, the fast train on column 444 is at (222,444) travelling upwards, so we can take this fast train and reach (111,444) at time 121212. Once again we are lucky and at time 121212 the fast train on row 111 is at (111,444), so we can take this fast train and reach (111, 111) at time 151515. An alternative route would be to take the ordinary train on column 333 from (222,333) at time 888 and reach (111,333) at time 101010. We then wait there till time 131313 and take the fast train on row 111 going left, reaching (111,111) at time 151515. You can verify that there is no way of reaching (111,111) earlier than that.

###Input:

The first line contains two integers MMM and NNN indicating the number rows and columns in the metro system. This is followed by MMM lines, lines 2,3,…,M+12, 3, …, M+12,3,…,M+1, describing the trains along the MMM rows. The first letter on each line is either F or O or S, indicating whether the train is a fast train, an ordinary train or a slow train. Following this, separated by a blank space, is an integer indicating the time at which this train starts running. The next NNN lines, lines M+2,M+3,…,N+M+1M+2, M+3, …, N+M+1M+2,M+3,…,N+M+1, contain similar descriptions of the trains along the NNN columns. The last line, line N+M+2N+M+2N+M+2, contains 555 integers a,b,c,da, b, c, da,b,c,d and eee where (aaa,bbb) is the starting station, ccc is the starting time and (ddd,eee) is the destination station.

###Output:

A single integer indicating the earliest time at which one may reach the destination.

###Constraints:


1≤M,N≤501 \leq M, N \leq 501≤M,N≤50.
1≤a,d≤M1 \leq a, d \leq M1≤a,d≤M
1≤b,e≤N1 \leq b, e \leq N1≤b,e≤N
1≤1 \leq1≤ all times in input ≤20\leq 20≤20

solution.approach: The idea to solve the problem is to simulate the movement of trains and calculate the shortest travel time using Breadth-First Search `(BFS)`. Each cell in the grid represents a metro station, and the `BFS` explores neighboring cells to calculate the minimum time to reach them. For each train, its position at a given time is calculated using a periodic function that accounts for its back-and-forth movement. When visiting a neighboring cell, the earliest time at which the train can arrive is computed and updated. `BFS` ensures that all possible paths are explored, and the minimum time to reach the destination is recorded. The logic revolves around systematically exploring all possible transitions, respecting train timings, and ensuring the shortest path is always prioritized.
